coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic activities of graphene quantum dots constructed from four different nitropyrenes on water redox reaction and organic pollutant degradation

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jingjing
Liu, Ruonan
Qiao, Yuxin
Liu, Shuxin
Qin, Chuanguang
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanometer-sized fragments of graphene with unique characters, which make them as new interesting application candidates in the fields of chemical, environmental and energy engineering. In this paper, the four nitropyrenes with different nitration degree, such as mononitropyrene, dinitropyrene, trinitropyrene and tetranitropyrene, were successfully synthesized and used to rationally construct corresponding graphite phase quantum dots named GQD(1), GQD(2), GQD(3) and GQD(4) in turn. Subsequently, the relationship between the structure and photocatalytic activity of different intermediates for the preparation of GQD were systematically studied. Degree of polymerization and lateral size of GQDs prepared with different intermediates significantly affected their photocatalytic performance. Through comparision of the photocatalytic water splitting reaction of four GQDs, it was found that GQD(4) had the best photocatalytic efficiency among four GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitropyrene
Graphene quantum dot (GQD)
Photocatalytic activity
Synthesis
Water treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic water splitting to produce hydrogen
H2O2 is produced by photodegradation of pollutants
Conflicts of interest or competing interests

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21572180), the Intergovernmental Science and Technology Cooperation and Exchange Program between the People's Republic of China and Romania (43-24-20180510), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (2012JZ2002), and Academic Funding of Northwestern Polytechnical University (NPU, 17GH0402, W212002). We gratefully acknowledge the Analytical and Testing Center (ATC) of Northwestern Polytechnical University for providing well-support as SEM, TEM and XRD throughout the research work.

This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21572180 ), the Intergovernmental Science and Technology Cooperation and Exchange Program between China and Romania ( 43-24-20180510 ), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China ( 2012JZ2002 ), and Academic Funding of Northwestern Polytechnical University (NPU, 17GH0402 , W212002 ). We gratefully acknowledge the Analytical and Testing Center (ATC) of Northwestern Polytechnical University for providing well-support as SEM, TEM and XRD throughout the research work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process of nitro compounds and GQDs.
Spectral characterization of GQDs. (a) The XRD patterns. (b) The FTIR patterns.
(a) High-resolution XPS spectra. (b) Atomic percentage. (c–e) High-resolution C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s XPS spectra of GQD(1), GQD(2), GQD(3) and GQD(4).
TEM images of GQDs prepared by (a) Monitropyrene, (b) Dinitropyrene, (c) Trinitropyrene, and (d) Tetritropyrene, respectively. (e) Particle size distribution histograms of GQDs.
Spectral characterization of GQDs. (a) UV–Vis patterns. (b) PL patterns.
Hydrogen production by photocatalytic water cracking of GQDs.

(a) Curves of photocatalytic H2O2 production by GQDs. (b) Photocatalytic H2O2 production of GQD(4) with isopropyl alcohol. (c-d) H2O2 curves of GQDs with isopropyl alcohol (pH=4 / 10). (e) photocatalytic H2O2 production cycle Curve of GQD(4).

(a) photocatalytic TC degradation Curves of GQDs. (b) Photocatalytic curves after adsorption removal.(c) the effect of trapping agent on photocatalytic degradation of TC. (d) photocatalytic degradation TC cycle of GQD(4). (e-f) XRD and FTIR patterns of fresh and used GQD(4).

(a) The effect of GQDs on the adsorption of MB with different doses of adsorbents (MB concentration: 40 mg/L, MB volume: 50 mL). (b) The effect of GQD(4) on the adsorption of MB by different concentrations (MB volume: 50 mL, adsorbent dosage: 10 mg). (c) Effect of GQD(4) with contact time on MB adsorption.
(a) The curve of H2O2 produced while MB was degraded. (b) Curve of H2O2 produced while TC was degraded.
The proposed charge transfer mechanism of GQD(4).
The photocatalytic hydrogen production performance of the prepared samples is compared with that of similar photocatalysts reported in the literature.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic activities of graphene quantum dots constructed from four different nitropyrenes on water redox reaction and organic pollutant degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Polymer Science and Technology, OME Key Laboratory of Supernormal Material Physics and Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi’an 710129, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Polymer Science and Technology
OME Key Laboratory of Supernormal Material Physics and Chemistry
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Polymer Science and Technology, OME Key Laboratory of Supernormal Material Physics and Chemistry, School of Natural and Applied Sciences, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi’an 710072, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanometer-sized fragments of graphene with unique characters, which make them as new interesting application candidates in the fields of chemical, environmental and energy engineering. In this paper, the four nitropyrenes with different nitration degree, such as mononitropyrene, dinitropyrene, trinitropyrene and tetranitropyrene, were successfully synthesized and used to rationally construct corresponding graphite phase quantum dots named GQD(1), GQD(2), GQD(3) and GQD(4) in turn. Subsequently, the relationship between the structure and photocatalytic activity of different intermediates for the preparation of GQD were systematically studied. Degree of polymerization and lateral size of GQDs prepared with different intermediates significantly affected their photocatalytic performance. Through comparision of the photocatalytic water splitting reaction of four GQDs, it was found that GQD(4) had the best photocatalytic efficiency among four GQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous consumption of energy in the world, there is a shortage of fossil fuels. The combustion of fossil fuels is also accompanied by environmental pollution, greenhouse effect, and other issues [1]. Therefore, finding a clean and pollution-free solution is an urgent challenge to deal with the environmental and energy problems of today. Photocatalysis utilizes inexhaustible, inexpensive solar energy as the main source of energy. Using solar energy to generate clean energy and degrade pollutants is considered a promising means [2]. In photocatalytic reactions, photoelectrons can be used as reducing agents to reduce hydrogen in water to hydrogen [3,4], oxygen to hydrogen peroxide [5–8], carbon dioxide to hydrocarbons [9], or Cr(VI) reduction [10,11]. Oxidation involves using holes to degrade organic compounds [12], splitting water into oxygen [13], and oxidizing water to hydrogen peroxide [14].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), consisting of single or few layers of graphene with transverse dimensions less than 10 nm, are a novel zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials [15–17]. Graphene is well known for its high mechanical strength, high stability, high electrical conductivity and a sizable specific surface area. Graphene quantum dots combine the advantages of graphene and quantum dots. Researchers have achieved a more perfect combination of graphene and quantum dots through various strategies such as designing quantum dots with different sizes, doping elements, and adding functional groups for modification, which can be applied in more fields. Compared to graphene, GQDs have better water solubility, coordinated band gaps, and significant quantum size effects [18]. Compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots (such as CdS, PbS, etc.), GQDs have better biocompatibility and are more environmentally friendly [19,20]. Based on the above advantages, GQDs have extensive applications in many different domains, including drug delivery, biological imaging, and photocatalysis [21–25].

Here, we have successfully prepared four different nitro compounds through nitration using inexpensive and low toxic pyrene (C16H10) as a precursor, and then successfully prepared four GQDs. Under visible light irradiation, we have efficiently photocatalytic water splitting to resolve H2, produce H2O2, and remove pollutants. Compared with the other three GQDs, the prepared GQD(4) has the highest photocatalytic efficiency. This work not only reported a new type of metal free catalyst with excellent photocatalytic performance, but also studied the relationship between the photocatalytic performance of GQD prepared by different intermediate systems.

Pyrene came from Shanghai McLean Biotechnology Co., nitric acid from Guangdong Guanghua Technology Co., and ammonia from Guangdong Guanghua Technology Co., Throughout all of the experiments, deionized water was utilized.

The reaction formula of nitro compound is shown in Fig. 1 . Characterization of nitro compounds is attached to supporting information.
(1)
Monitropyrene: 2.00 g (10 mmol, purity > 98 %) pyrene was first added into the beeper as raw material, an appropriate amount of acetic acid was added as solvent, and 10 mmol concentrated nitric acid (purity: 68 %) was added at molar ratio 1:1, Stirring for two hours. Distilled water was used to neutralize the solution when the reaction was complete. dried at 60 ℃ and ground to obtain 2.09 g (85 % yield) yellow monitropyrene compound.
(2)
Dinitropyrene: 2.00 g (10 mmol, purity > 98 %) pyrene was weighed into the flask, and an appropriate amount of glacial acetic acid was added as the solvent, followed by 30 mmol concentrated nitric acid, and stirred for 6 h at 50 °C. Following the reaction liquid's cooling to ambient temperature and distilled water neutralization, it was dried at 60 °C and ground to obtain 2.54 g (87 % yield) yellow dinitropyrene compound.
(3)
Trinitropyrene: 2.00 g (10 mmol, purity > 98 %) pyrene was weighed into a flask, after adding 160 ml of concentrated nitric acid, the mixture is stirred for 12 h at 80 °C. Once the temperature has dropped to room temperature, rinsed the solution to neutral with distilled water. The residue was dried at 60 ℃ and ground to obtain 3.05 g (91 % yield) of yellow trinitropyrene compound.
(4)
Tetranitropyrene: 2.00 g (10 mmol, purity > 98 %) pyrene was weighed into a flask, then 200 mL concentrated nitric acid was added to it and stirred at 100 ℃ for 16 h. Distilled water was used to neutralize it once the temperature was brought down to room temperature. dried at 60 °C and ground to obtain 3.45 g (90 % yield) of yellow tetritropyrene compound.

4 Mmol of nitropyrene compound was weighed, and 220.60 mL distilled water and 19.40 mL concentrated ammonia water (1.20 M) were added to the compound, and it is ultrasonicated for two hours in an ice bath. Then heated at 200 °C in a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor for 10 h. Cool and filter to remove insoluble material. About 220 mL of water was steamed using a rotary evaporator at 80 ℃. The remaining solution was further dialyzed until the dialyzed water appeared colorless, The concentrated mixture was put into a flask with a round bottom, dried at 80 ℃ and further ground to obtain the black product, namely graphene quantum dots (GQD). The four types of graphene quantum dots are denoted as GQD(1), GQD(2), GQD(3) and GQD(4)

The composite's crystal structure was analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (D8) with Cu-Kα radiation. The microstructure of the samples was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Talos F200X). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD) using Al Kα radiation was used to obtain the surface chemical properties of the material. The absorbance of the samples was tested by UV-1800 instrument made by Shanghai Elite Technology Instrument Co., LTD. PL was carried out using an F-7100 fluorescence spectrometer from Hitachi, Japan. The EIS of the samples were tested with the CHI660E instrument of Shanghai Chenhua Company. BET was performed using 3H-2000PS2 instrument from Beijing Best Company, with the following parameters: above 0.0005 (m2/g) (specific surface area); 2 nm-500 nm (mesopore and macroporous analysis); 0.35 nm−2 nm (microporous routine analysis). FTIR was tested using a Nicolet iS50 instrument from Thermo Fly. The samples were tested using Avance 400 MHz instrument of Bruker, Germany. The sample was tested using the Q-Exactive model instrument of Thermo Scientific in the United States to determine its molecular weight.

The evaluation process of photocatalytic performance of aquatic hydrogen by different photocatalysts is as follows. Weigh 0.01 g catalyst and put it in 40 mL aqueous solution containing isopropyl alcohol, the proportion of isopropyl alcohol is 5 %, ultrasonic 10 min to make the photocatalyst fully dispersed in the solution. The quartz tube containing catalyst and solution is sealed with a rubber plug and sealed with a sealing film. A 600 W xenon lamp (visible light) was selected as the light source. The agitator was turned on before the light was turned on, and the air in the reactor was discharged. After repeated readings by drainage and gas collection, the light was turned on and the reaction began. The change in value was recorded by reading it every 1 h. The reaction lasted for 3 h.

To evaluate the photocatalytic performance of aquatic H2O2 using different photocatalysts, the following process is employed: 0.04 g of the photocatalyst is ultrasonically separated in 50 mL of water and stirred for 0.5 h in the absence of light to ensure full contact between the solution and air. Light for the photocatalytic is provided by a 600 W xenon lamp. Throughout the illumination period, 2 mL solution is sampled per hour, and took out catalyst by filter. The concentration of H2O2 is determined through iodometry.1 mL collected solution is putted into 5 mL tube. Subsequently, 1 mL of 0.1 mol-L-1 aqueous potassium hydrogen phthalate (C8H5KO4) and 1 mL of 0.4 mol-L-1 aqueous potassium iodide (KI) were added [26].After 30 min, the absorbance of the solution at 350 nm was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The H2O2 yield is then calculated based on the obtained absorbance value.

The performance evaluation process of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants using different photocatalysts is as follows. Disperse 0.01 g of the photocatalyst in a 40 mL solution containing pollutants. Start magnetic stirring and stir uniformly for 0.5 h in the absence of light to achieve adsorption equilibrium in the photocatalytic system. The photocatalytic process should then be carried out with a 600 W xenon lamp and sample 4 mL of the solution every 1 h. Filter the extracted reaction solution and use UV–Vis spectrophotometer to measure the filtrate. Determine the rate of degradation by utilizing the absorbance at the wavelength of maximum absorption.

The performance evaluation process of different photocatalysts for photocatalytic adsorption of pollutants is as follows. The 40 mL MB solution was used to disperse the 0.01 g photocatalyst and magnetic stirrer was started to make the catalyst fully contact with MB, So as to complete the catalyst's purification and adsorption of pollutants.

NMR (Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy) was used to characterize the structures of GQDs prepared by the pair and the findings are displayed in Fig. S1. The graph displays the NMR diagrams of four nitro compounds, Fig. S1(a and b) shows the hydrogen and carbon spectra of mononitropyrene, Fig. S1(c and d) shows the hydrogen and carbon spectra of dinitropyrene, Fig.S1(e and f) shows the hydrogen and carbon spectra of trinitropyrene, and Fig.S1(g and h) shows the hydrogen and carbon spectra of tetranitropyrene. Comparison with the NMR diagrams of the standard samples proved that the nitro compounds were successfully prepared.The high-resolution mass spectra of mononitropyrene, dinitropyrene, trinitropyrene, and tetranitropyrene are shown in Fig. S2(a–d), respectively. The calculated values of molecular weights and the molecular weights measured in the positive ion mode were almost the same, proving that the target products were successfully prepared.

XRD (X-ray powder diffraction), FTIR (Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy) and XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) spectra was used to further illustrate the successful preparation of compounds. Fig. 2 a is the XRD pattern of the samples, and it is found that the four GQDs all have an obvious diffraction peak at about 26°. Comparison of standard XRD cards revealed that the diffraction peaks corresponded to the (002) crystal facet of the GQD, with a crystal layer spacing of 3.34 Å. similar to that of the massive graphite, indicating that the highly graphitized GQD has been successfully prepared [27]. Since there are no additional peaks, the GQD is determined to be composed of a single layer of high-functional carbon with non-static properties. Fig. 2b displays the infrared spectra of four graphene quantum dots. The carbon–carbon double bond (C=C) vibration is represented by the absorption peak at 1590 cm−1, the bending vibration of N-H is represented by the absorption peak at 3200 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1, and the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group (–OH) is represented by the absorption peak at approximately 3400 cm-1. The primary amide's C-N stretching vibration is responsible for the absorption peak near 1407 cm-1, the absorption peak near 1105 cm−1 is attributed to the vibrations of C-N and the bending vibration of C-OH is responsible for the absorption peak at 1270 cm−1 [28]. This result indicated that GQDs synthesized in the presence of ammonia was successfully functionalized by ammonia.

In order to further verify the successful introduction of amino group in GQDs, the XPS spectra of the four quantum dots were tested. The materials all contain C、N、O elements in Fig. 3 (a). And it is known from the elemental content analysis in Fig. 3.

(b) that with the increase of nitro group, GQD(1) to GQD(4), the content of C element in the material decreases (82.18–76.27 %), while the content of nitrogen element increases (4.63–9.94 %). Elemental oxygen may be partly introduced by adsorption during the preparation of the material. Based on the comprehensive analysis of Fig. 3c–e, the peak around aroud 285 eV in C 1 s is considered to be the C-C bond of graphene sp2 hybridization [29,30], while the characteristic peak around 286 eV corresponds to the C-N bond, the pyridine nitrogen introduced at the edge of the quantum dots after the reaction [19,28].

As alkaline hydrothermal conditions promote denitration and dehydrogenation of nitro compounds, the subsequent covalent attachment of the resulting dangling carbon bonds leads to fusion between the sp2 precursors. We hypothesized that a suitable sp2:sp3 ratio(1:1) in GQD(4) could lead to a homogeneous, smaller lateral size structure with more edge reactive groups [31,32].

As the materials are prepared in ammonia solution, the edge sites are functionalized by OH and NH2, which is verified by around 287 eV in C 1 s and aroud 397 eV in N 1 s and 528 eV in O 1 s. Moreover, the ratio of NH2 / pyridinic N rises as the number of nitro groups increases and more NH2 is introduced. In addition, by the characteristic peak of 529 eV in O 1 s, it can be found that there are some –NO2 residues.

The TEM image in Fig. 4 shows graphene quantum dots well dispersed and have uniform transverse dimensions. The lamellar structure around the GQD prepared by monitropyrene suggests that monitropyrene has only one active site and its degree of polymerization is not enough to produce only a small amount of GQD. Compared with GQD(1) and GQD(2), GQD(3) and GQD(4) had higher polymerization degree and significantly improved yield. The transverse dimensions of GQD(1), GQD(2), GQD(3) and GQD(4) are about 1.5–––6.5 nm, 0.75–––5.75 nm, 1.25–––4.25 nm, and 1.25–––3.75 nm. The transverse size of the produced GQD decreases as the amount of nitro in the precursor increases.The nitrogen doping at high concentrations may produce small quantum dots with particle size and narrow distribution that can be interpreted as the contribution of nitrogen to the enhanced surface defect state. The increase in surface defect states might create more tailoring sites.

UV–Vis of four nitro compounds was explored in N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) at room temperature to test the optical properties of the compounds. As shown in Fig. S3(a), the four nitro pyrene showed maximum absorption peaks at 404 nm, 408 nm, 416 nm and 417 nm, respectively because of the increasing number of nitro groups. The fluorescence spectra of the four nitro compounds were explored with N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) at room temperature to test the optical properties of the compounds. As shown in Fig. S3(b), the fluorescence of four nitropyrene compounds was excited with 414 nm, 418 nm, 426 nm, and 427 nm, yielding the maximum fluorescence intensity at 460 nm, 490.6 nm, 502.6 nm, and 505.4 nm.

The UV absorption visible spectra of four graphene quantum dots were investigated at room temperature with water as solvent (250 mg/L). As shown in Fig. 5 (a), their absorption spectra show three obvious exciton absorption bands, with the center at about 261 nm, 302 nm and 510 nm. The absorption of GQD drops sharply after 510 nm, and the optical absorption edge is ∼ 600 nm, showing good absorption of visible light. The absorption peak at 261 nm is a result of the π → π* and σ → π* transitions in the aromatic sp2 region, the excitation peak at 302 nm corresponds to the n → π* transition of the oxygen-containing functional group of C-O-C, and the absorption peak at 510 nm is due to the surface state transition [19]. Fig. 5(b) shows the photoluminescence spectra of GQDs excited at 510 nm. The figure illustrates that the absorption wavelength is around 568 nm and has the maximum absorption intensity. It is found that the quantum dot size increases resulted in a red shift of fluorescence emission, and the larger the size same as the larger the conjugated system, exhibits increased fluorescence intensity. [33] Compared with the other three GQDs, GQD (4) showed the lowest fluorescence intensity, indicating a lower recombination rate of photogigenic electrons and holes, efficient charge separation, and possibly exhibiting a higher photocatalytic activity.

In order to further understand the ability of the catalyst to perform photocaller charge transfer and analyze the separation ability of photogenerated carriers, electrochemical impedance tests were conducted. In general, the smaller the semicircle in the Nyquist diagram, the lower the charge transfer resistance. The result in Fig. 6 shows that GQD(4) possessed almost faster charge transfer, because it has a smaller arc radius and a smaller resistance as same as GQD(3), thus speeding up the electron transmission speed [34–36]. It is further proved that GQD(4) has strong photocharge separation and transfer efficiency and reactivity to visible light.

Additionally, one of the more significant variables influencing the photocatalytic activity is the specific surface area. Fig. S4 displays the catalysts' nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms. More active sites can be supplied in proportion to the photocatalysts' specific surface area and pore volume, which in turn makes the reactants easier to be transported and separated, thus increasing their photocatalytic activity. The specific surface areas of the four GQDs were 4.03, 4.63, 6.84, and 7.66 m2/g. From the figure, it can be seen that all four GQDs had type IV isotherms, indicating the presence of mesoporous structures in the materials, and their hysteresis isotherms were all of type H3, indicating that the pore structures in the materials were highly irregular [37,38]. The pore sizes of the four GQDs are mainly around 0–5 nm, which further confirms that the material is mesoporous structure [39]. With the increase of nitro groups in the nitro compounds. The effectively produced GQDs had a higher specific surface area, which might offer more active sites for photocatalytic reactions and effective pathways for reactant transportation. The structure improves the photocatalytic activity by introducing the separation of electron-hole pairs and facilitating effective light energy harvesting. The BET parameters of the four GQDs are shown in Table S1.

To investigate these four GQDs' photocatalytic properties, water cracking was performed to produce H2, H2O2, adsorption MB and degradation TC by GQD, and the photocatalytic activities of these four GQDs were compared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic water splitting to produce hydrogen
ce:section.ce:para: Four GQDs were used to test the performance of photocatalytic water cracking for hydrogen production, and Fig. 7 presents the findings. As the image illustrates, the photocatalytic activity of GQD(4) is relatively good. Under visible light irradiation, the hydrogen production efficiency within 3 h is up to 146.81 μmol•g−1•h−1, followed by the photocatalytic activity of GQD(3) and GQD(2). The photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiencies of GQD(1) were 111.30 μmol•g−1•h−1 and 85.83 μmol•g−1•h−1, respectively. In the identical testing circumstances, the hydrogen production efficiencies of GQD(1) were the lowest, 50.32 μmol•g−1•h−1. In order to clearly understand the hydrogen production efficiency of GQD prepared by various methods, Table 1 summarizes the hydrogen production results of some catalysts similar to GQD(such as GO, CD, etc.). It is found that the photocatalytic H2 production activity of prepared GQD(4), GQD(3) and GQD(2) is in the middle among most previously reported catalysts. The results show that the prepared GQD catalyst is a promising catalytic material.

To further systematically evaluate the photocatalytic performance, the photocatalytic production of H2O2 was tested on the prepared photocatalyst, and Fig. 8 presents the findings. As the image illustrates, GQD(1), GQD(2) and GQD(3) produce 210.32, 212.24 and 216.10 μM H2O2 respectively within 6 h of visible light irradiation. In contrast, GQD(4) shows better H2O2 generation performance (244.13 μM) under the same conditions. The H2O2 generation rate is about 1.16 times that of GQD(1). In order to explore the influence of adding sacrificial agent (isopropyl alcohol) and different pH on its photocatalytic activity. Under the same test conditions, isopropyl alcohol (v/v = 5 %) was added into the aqueous solution as a sacrificant to test the photocatalytic activity. The figure shows that the addition of sacrificant can improve the photocatalytic effect, and the H2O2 produced by GQD(4) can reach 283.71 μM, which is 1.16 times of the photocatalytic rate without sacrificant. The pH of H2SO4 and NaOH solution was adjusted to 4 and 10 respectively. The photocatalytic performance was tested under the same test conditions. It was found that the H2O2 generation rate was significantly enhanced when pH=10. GQD(1), GQD(2), GQD(3), GQD(4) produced 418.53, 504.01, 546.04, 662.02 μM H2O2 within 6 h of visible light irradiation, respectively. However, when the pH is adjusted to 4, the formation rate of H2O2 decreases instead of increasing. More importantly, considering that stability is a very important property of photocatalyst, owing to GQD's good photocatalytic activity(4), repeated experiments were conducted on the catalyst to determine its stability, and Fig. 8e displayed the findings. Notably, following three successive cycles, the H2O2 producing activity did not substantially decline, indicating that the prepared GQD(4) samples had good recyclability and physicochemical stability.

The four GQDs were used to conduct the photocatalytic degradation experiment of tetracycline (TC) pollutants, and Fig. 9 displays the test findings. The photocatalytic degradation TC curve, which includes dark adsorption, is depicted in Fig. 9(a–b). The image illustrates that within 30 min of dark adsorption, the adsorption rate of GQD(4) on TC is 11.42 %, followed by the adsorption efficiency of GQD(2) and GQD(3), whose adsorption rates are 0.91 % and 0.73 % respectively, and The lowest adsorption capacity is seen in GQD(1). Fig. 9(b) shows the photocatalytic degradation after removal of adsorption capacity. The graphic illustrates the degradation rate of GQD (4) within 4 h is 22.02 %, GQD(3) within 4 h is 20.11 %, GQD(2) within 4 h is 15.72 %, and GQD(1) has the worst photocatalytic degradation performance. The degradation rate was 13.44 % in 4 h.

To explore the role of the active species in the photocatalytic degradation of TC, free radical quenching experiments were performed [35]. In experiments, AgNO3 and EDTA (2 mM) were used to capture electron (e-) and hole (h+), respectively, and to add IPA (1.0 M) and BQ (4.3 mM) as capture agents for OH– and ·O2 –. Fig. 9(c) shows that the degradation of TC was inhibited after the binding of AgNO3 and BQ, but the rate of photocatalytic degradation was basically unchanged after the addition of IPA, while the addition of EDTA inhibited it to some extent. Since oxygen and photoelectron react to form·O2 –, this implies that the main active species of the system should be ·O2 –.

The stability of the photocatalytic activity is an important indicator to measure the practical application value of the catalyst. The results of cycling experiments for photodegraded TC are shown in Fig. 9(d). After three consecutive cycles, the TC degradation rate did not decrease significantly. It indicates good stability and reutilization. In Fig. 9(e–f), the diffraction peak position and intensity of XRD of GQD (4) did not change significantly before and after the cycle experiment, indicating that the crystal structure of the material was not damaged and extremely stable after three cycles. Moreover, the infrared spectrum of the sample did not change significantly, indicating a good chemical and structural stability of the sample.

Since GQD has a good adsorption performance for MB, by adding different amounts of GQD material to MB solution (40 mg/L), the influence of adsorbents of different qualities on the adsorption efficiency was tested. It was found that when the amount of adsorbents increased, Firstly, the adsorption efficiency of MB rose; thereafter, it nearly stayed constant, reached a balance, as shown in Fig. 10 (a). It is evident that the GQD material produced inthis paperhas a potent ability to adsorb MB, and its dosage has no discernible impact on MB's rate of adsorption. Even a tiny amount of adsorbent can result in a higher adsorption rate. Depending on the catalyst's cost and removal efficiency, 10 mg material was selected for subsequent experiments. All four kinds of GQDs have good adsorption properties for MB, while GQD(4) has relatively high photocatalytic activity. Therefore, GQD(4) is used as adsorbent to conduct subsequent adsorption experiments. Using MB solutions with various concentrations (20 mg/L, 50 mg/L, 70 mg/L, 100 mg/L) for adsorption performance tests, it is found that when the concentration of MB increases, to get past the adsorbent's and the solution's mass transfer resistance, the adsorbent requires a stronger driving force [46]. As the concentration of MB increases, the adsorption rate of MB decreases, possibly due to the adsorbent's active center reaching saturation at a specific concentration, as shown in Fig. 10(b). Next, it was investigated how contact time affected the adsorption characteristics. It was discovered that as the duration of contact increased, the adsorption capacity increased and reached equilibrium 40 min later, as demonstrated in Fig. 10(c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2O2 is produced by photodegradation of pollutants
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic reaction of MB and TC solution (10 mg/L) produced H2O2 at the same time of degradation, and Fig. 11 displayed the outcome. The outcomes demonstrated that GQD(4) performed the best in terms of photocatalysis, and GQD(1) had the lowest photocatalytic activity. Within 4 h of visible light irradiation, the photocatalysis of four kinds of photocatalysts in MB solution produced 10.82, 12.01, 12.43, 13.70 μM H2O2, respectively. The content of H2O2 produced by photocatalysis in TC solution was significantly higher than that in MB solution, and the photocatalysis results were 35.52, 45.51, 51.34, 60.52 μM, respectively.

Therefore, based on the above experimental results and analysis, combined with the UV–vis DRS, we know that the band gap of GQD(4) is 1.35 eV. Thus the VB of GQD(4)and CB of GQD(4)were calculated to be 0.98eV and −0.37 eV by the xps valence band spectrum in Fig. S5. We speculate that the main photocatalytic degradation mechanism of GQD(4) in Fig. 12 , under visible light, photoelectrons are excited from the valence band to the conduction band, and holes are left in the valence band.photoelectrons can produce ∙O2 – with O2 on the adsorbed GQD(4) surface. ∙O2 – with oxidation capacity oxidizes TC to produce small molecular material; Photoelectrons can also produce hydrogen by photocatalytic reduction reaction with water. The photoelectrons and holes in GQD(4) activate oxygen and water molecules respectively, and finally produce a two-electron reduction reaction between hydrogen proton and oxygen provided by water molecules to form hydrogen peroxide.Based on the above analysis, we propose the charge transfer and reaction process as follows:
(1)
GQD
4
+
h ν
→
GQD
4
e
-
+
h
+
(2)
O
2
+
e
-
→ ·
O
2
-
(3)
2 H
+
+
2 e
-
→
H
2
(4)
·
O
2
-
+
TC
→
Prod
(5)
2 H
+
+
O
2
+
2 e
-
→
H
2
O
2

In this work, mononitropyrene, dinitropyrene, trinitropyrene and tetranitropyrene compounds were successfully prepared by different reaction conditions, and then using them as precursors, four kinds of GQDs containing amino groups were successfully prepared by aqueous fusion technology. The diffraction peaks, compositions, morphologies and chemical states of the GQDs were then tested by various characterization methods. The experimental findings demonstrated that each of the four GQDs has a distinct photocatalytic activity. It was discovered that the photocatalytic efficiency of the four GQDs was GQD(4) > GQD(3) > GQD(2) > GQD(1) after comparing the photocatalytic activity of the aforementioned photocatalysts. One possible explanation for GQD(4)'s superior performance over other morphologies in creating H2 and H2O2 could be its thin thickness and big specific surface area. We think that this catalyst material will offer a straightforward and long-term approach to the development of water purification technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conflicts of interest or competing interests
ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.
Not applicable.
Not applicable.
Not applicable.

Jingjing Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ruonan Liu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Yuxin Qiao: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Shuxin Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Chuanguang Qin: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21572180), the Intergovernmental Science and Technology Cooperation and Exchange Program between the People's Republic of China and Romania (43-24-20180510), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (2012JZ2002), and Academic Funding of Northwestern Polytechnical University (NPU, 17GH0402, W212002). We gratefully acknowledge the Analytical and Testing Center (ATC) of Northwestern Polytechnical University for providing well-support as SEM, TEM and XRD throughout the research work.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.flatc.2024.100735.

ce:section.ce:para:
